segment-text: Paul Greengrass and Luke Davies
EXT. WICHITA FALLS - EVENING
An isolated cluster of distant shanties. Caption: North Texas, February 1870
EXT. WICHITA FALLS - CONTINUOUS
See the town closer now. In the shadow of a turning windmill.
EXT. MAIN STREET. WICHITA FALLS - CONTINUOUS
A wind-beaten Main Street.
EXT. BUILDING. WICHITA FALLS - CONTINUOUS
A wall of posters. Faded lists of Civil War dead. Bodies still unaccounted for.
EXT. LIVERY BARN - CONTINUOUS - NIGHT PEOPLE heading into the Livery Barn.
In the foreground, a printed sign on a wooden post: The Latest News and Articles. From the Major Journals of the Civilised World. A Compendium Read by:
Livery Barn INT. LIVERY BARN - CONTINUOUS A makeshift stage.
On it a table, a bull-lamp and a small tin pot. PEOPLE throw coins into the pot as they pass.
INT. BACKSTAGE. LIVERY BARN - CONTINUOUS CAPTAIN JEFFERSON KYLE KIDD (63) dressing.

As he puts on his shirt and dress jacket, we glimpse several bullet scars. He adjusts his neck-tie in front of a shard of mirror. His is a face that's seen life. Death too. Three wars to be precise. He takes a moment to gather himself. A lonely storyteller, searching for peace. Then picks up a leather portfolio. Steps out onto stage.
INT. LIVERY BARN - CONTINUOUS

Kidd lights the bull-lamp. Unfastens the portfolio. Inside, newspapers. He lays them out on the table. We glimpse headlines: Civil War over. End of Slavery. He looks out at his audience. Every face a story of hardscrabble life. Relentless struggle.

So let me do that work for you. And maybe just for tonight, we can escape our troubles and hear of the great changes a'happening out there. (adjusts his newspapers) Now, for any virgin listeners, here's how it's gonna go: We'll start with a little local news. That which affects you and yours truly most direct. From there we'll go on and take in the federal picture... GROANS from audience.

Oh I hear ya, you know I do, they got us down and now they're keeping us down... But lend me your ears even still. Cos, after we've heard what those wise, wise gentlemen up in Washington have in store for us next... (laughter) Well then we get to take wings and escape... (holding up The Bombay Gazette) We'll head to the crimson mountains of India in pursuit of a marauding beast that's claimed the lives of thirty-one farming souls. And it's still a-hunting... (holds up another paper) (MORE)
Then we'll sweep west into deepest Africa by way of the pages of
Harpers Illustrated, and pick up
explorer, battling through the
And we see his audience hooked.

Starting local then: The Houston Telegraph reports, February 1st: (begins to read) "The Meningitis epidemic continues to spread without prejudice across the Panhandle and North Texas region. So far it has claimed ninety-seven souls, in a two-month period. However, this figure is likely higher, as families fail to report the deaths of..."
INT. LIVERY BARN - NIGHT

So we got our delegation from the state of Texas up there in Washington DC. Commencing those talks about rejoining the Union. And speaking of the Union, the Dallas Herald reports, (starts reading) "a Mr Charles Porter, Clerk to the House of Representatives" no less, has been caught with his paw in the cookie jar, "charged with embezzling six thousand dollars of public money..." (stops reading) The majority of which seems to have been spent wining and dining a Miss Dolores Flynn, at Washington DC's very fine Willard Hotel... Good to know those negotiations are well underway then...
INT. LIVERY BARN - NIGHT
Kidd reaches the climax of his reading.
the upper hand. With his free arm he reached towards his fallen blade. The jaws of the monster
his fingers around the hilt and as
the mighty tiger fell silent." Kidd sets the newspaper down. The audience bursts into APPLAUSE.
INT/EXT. LIVERY BARN - NIGHT
Kidd packing his newspapers back into the portfolio. MALE SPECTATOR Mighty fine reading, Captain.
You come back soon.
Kidd walks away towards his boarding house.
INT. KIDD'S ROOM. BOARDING HOUSE - NIGHT
Kidd at the window, undoing his collar. Looking at the street below. His newspapers on a table behind him. And a locket. A faded photograph of a woman. Spanish-looking, beautiful.
EXT. WICHITA FALLS - SUNRISE
Wide shot of the town.
EXT. LIVERY STABLES - EARLY MORNING
Kidd saddles his HORSES. Takes the road out of town.
EXT. ROAD TO RED RIVER - DAY
Kidd alone in the landscape. The grandeur of Texas. Immense. Inhospitable.
EXT. A SHALLOW VALLEY. RED RIVER ROAD - DAY

Kidd enters a shallow valley. The track narrower. Flanked by trees and dense thicket. The atmosphere shifts. A creeping sense of menace. Kidd alert. Watching the shadows. A curve in the track ahead... Rounding it, he sees: A Wagon. Badly damaged. An axle broken. No sign of the horses. Kidd dismounts. The wagon is empty. And a cluster of hoof marks in the dust. And blood. The signs of someone having been dragged away. Kidd takes out a BATTERED OLD SHOTGUN from behind his saddle. He follows the trail... And finds an African-America Man, strung up in a tree. Pinned to his chest a newspaper:
The wind in the trees. Then movement off to the side... A GIRL. She runs. He goes after her. Crashing through the thicket. Chase... Stop!
KIDD (CONT'D)
He reaches her. She struggles furiously. Bites his arm. Breaks free. More chasing... Until finally, he corners her.
I'm not gonna hurt you!
He slings his gun over his shoulder. Holds up his hands. No reaction.
Can you speak English? Nothing.
You know Plains sign? (signing) Friend. (then in English) You can't stay out here. Not safe. Suddenly, the girl launches into Kiowa tongue:
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Home. I don't speak Indian.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) I want to go home. I don't understand.
He holds out a hand. She watches his hand all the way. It's OK... Before he can touch her, she moves past him.
EXT. A SHALLOW VALLEY. RED RIVER ROAD - CONTINUOUS
They move back through the trees... Kidd putting himself between her and the body. Eyes forward. Don't look. They reach the wagon. Kidd rummages inside. Finds the Girl's Indian Agency papers.
OK, got your papers... Says here your name is Leonberger. (looks at her) Johanna Leonberger. That your name? Johanna? No reaction.

Suddenly, a NOISE down the track. The thunder of approaching hooves... Johanna terrified. See Kidd. Readying his gun... The thunder reaches a climax... Johanna darts under the wagon... And a column of FEDERAL SOLDIERS round the bend ahead. Kidd puts the shotgun back under his saddle... The LIEUTENANT calls the column to halt and rides alongside. Menace.
Got your loyalty oath? Kidd hands it over.
Soldiers begin searching Kidd's horses.
(studying his papers) Where'd you serve, Captain?
3rd Texas Infantry. Surrendered at Galveston, May 26th, 1865. You carrying?
Just an old twelve gauge. For birds.
A Soldier holds up the twelve-gauge. Inspects the breach.
The Soldier pulls out the wad. Pours birdshot into his palm.
Kidd points to the body. The Lieutenant looks over at the body and the girl.
I think he was transporting this little girl here for the Federal authorities. The Lieutenant reads. (hands papers back) You're good.
He prepares to move the company out.
And they ride off down the track. On Kidd. Left alone. Johanna staring at him from under the wagon.
EXT. GRAVE. SHALLOW VALLEY. RED RIVER ROAD - LATE AFTERNOON18
Kidd digging a grave. Hard, physical work. Johanna watching him.
EXT. SHALLOW VALLEY. RED RIVER ROAD - LATER
Kidd prepares the horses.
Johanna doesn't move. Kidd mounts his horse. Gestures for her to climb up. Still she doesn't move.
Suit yourself... He walks his horses on...
Kidd ahead of her. The wrecked wagon and grave behind.
A beat, And then she follows.
EXT. RED RIVER ROAD - LATER
As the sun gets low, they walk on. It starts to rain.
EXT. MAKE-SHIFT CAMP. RED RIVER ROAD - NIGHTFALL
Kidd lights a sheet-iron stove. Cuts a piece of bacon. Puts it on the heat. Looks over at Johanna. Sees she's watching. He holds out a piece.
She takes it, and eats. Kidd takes out the Indian Agency papers.
Says the Agent got you back when Kiowa were cleared out of
(keeps reading) They took you way down in Hill Country. Your parents and sister were... (the detail obviously shocking) (MORE)

Well they passed on... But you still got an aunt and uncle down there. Near Castroville. (registers the name) I know Castroville... Used to travel that way before the war... It's a German community. Farmers. Hard working.
Johanna eating. Doesn't reply.
Six years, huh?
Kidd looks at her distant expression.
Kannst du Deutsch sprechen? No reaction.
Kannst du... deine Familie...er.. remember?
He gives up. Lies back on his upturned saddle.
Well tomorrow we'll find someone to take you home.
He signs 'Home'. Johanna reads the sign but doesn't react. He pulls his coat up around his neck. And they both wait for the peace of sleep.
EXT. TEXAN LANDSCAPE - DAY
The immense Red River Valley
EXT. RED RIVER STATION - SUNSET
A cluster of buildings ahead. RED RIVER STATION. And beyond it the great Red River, marking the border with
EXT. MAIN STREET. RED RIVER STATION - CONTINUOUS Kidd and Johanna down Main Street.
Chaos. HERDS OF LONG HORN and lines of loaded freight WAGONS jam the street. The river crossing is closed. Tempers are rising. Ahead: a military command post. Tents, FEDERAL SOLDIERS.
INT. MILITARY COMMAND POST. RED RIVER - MOMENTS LATER
Kidd enters.
Kidd approaches the OVERWORKED DUTY OFFICER. Shows his oath papers.

Well I found this child, see. Kiowa had her. The Agent was paying a freighter to take her back. But he didn't make it. The Lieutenant patrolling the road told me to bring her here. What do you expect me to do? She needs taking home. Well the Agent's not back for three months. (hands papers back) Looks like you'll have to take her. I can't take her. I gotta work. I move around... Sort'a thing y'all here for, ain't it? Listen, friend. We got lost folks all over. Folks trying to find folks. Folks trying not to get found... (beat) So why don't you wait for the Agent. Or take her yourself.
Before Kidd can protest the Officer has already moved on to the NEXT PERSON.
EXT. MILITARY COMMAND POST. RED RIVER - MOMENTS LATER
Back outside, Kidd looks at Johanna. Now what? He sees in the distance: A run-down church. A REVEREND out front, clearing weeds.
EXT. CHURCH. RED RIVER - CONTINUOUS
Kidd approaches.
(calling over) Obliged if I could take the church tonight, Reverend?
EXT. CHURCH. RED RIVER - CONTINUOUS
Kidd hammering up a poster, advertising a reading. Writes underneath:
Church hall
As the SOUND of A SOLITARY FIDDLE drifts down the street.
EXT. GENERAL STORE - MOMENTS LATER

Kidd follows the sound of the fiddle. Reaches a small store. Outside, Long Horn cattle roam and cry. Inside, a handful of LITTLE GIRLS practice ballet. Badly. Taught by an IRISH WOMAN (late 40's), MRS BOUDLIN. Accompanied by her husband MR BOUDLIN (late 40's) on the fiddle. Kidd knocks on the window. Boudlin looks up. Johanna watches the girls behind the glass, staring at her.
(comes to the door) Hey Kidd, how you doing? Sees Johanna. She yours?
EXT. CAMP. EDGE OF RED RIVER STATION - EVENING
Kidd leads the horses and Johanna to a spot behind the church. The Boudlin's with them.
Three months, huh?
That's what he said
Well shit Kidd, what you gonna do?
I don't know. Wait for the Agent I guess.
Johanna sits in the dirt, as Kidd sets up camp.
Look at them blue eyes (kneeling down) Hello child, I'm Mrs Boudlin... You're safe now, the Good Lord has seen to it. We just need to clean you up a bit don't we?
Johanna looks at her with suspicion.
(also looking) Sure got a strange look about her, ain't she?
Speak any English, child?
Kidd finishes the tarpaulin. Turns.
I don't mean to bring you my troubles, Mrs Boudlin. But can I leave the girl with you? While I read. Truth is I'm laid pretty low, news business being what it is.
(tries to stroke Johanna) We can watch her, can't we Mr Boudlin?
(not so sure) Well, I guess
I'm obliged, Mrs Boudlin. (turns to Johanna) See here child, I have to go to work. These good people are going to watch you.
He signs: 'Friend.' She looks at Kidd blankly.
Well shit Kidd, she don't understand a damn thing.
Kidd picks up his portfolio and heads into town.
EXT. CHURCH. RED RIVER - NIGHT
Angle on the Church.
Good evening folks! Name's Captain Kidd and I'm here with all your news. But - unless y'all here for the first time or just stone-cold drunk - I'm thinking y'already knew that...
INT. CHURCH. RED RIVER - CONTINUOUS
Kidd in front of a restive audience of CATTLE-MEN and FREIGHTERS.
Now... I see some fellow out-oftowners before me. On the road to getting some place else. Or wishing you were at least... FEDERAL SOLDIERS stand at the back, watching for trouble.

(sees them) And I see you gentlemen at the back also. Far from home, minding we don't stir up trouble again... (looks down at his newspapers) Seems to me, what with the rains, everyone's a little out of sorts tonight. (turns a page) Well, the stories in these pages before me, they can't get us all home. They can't stop the rain. Or clear the roads... Damn, all they do is lie there and ask to be read. (beat) But then, maybe in reading them, we'll find ourselves traveling all the same. Maybe not for real, but in our imaginings. To some place else. Maybe some better place yet to come... Least ways, that's the hope. (beat) Ok, let's start local with the Carthage Banner, reporting page (reading) "The Red River ferry is sunk near Cross Timbers, and waters still too high to cross there." (stops reading) Same here, of course. And as for the roads, (reading) "Routes in all directions are bad. Between Gainsville and Sherman; on the southeastern towards Weatherford; and parts down to Elm Creek, completely washed out."
Yanks sending soldiers too blue to muddy their boots, that's why. Agreement in the crowd.
Quit your airs now. (picks up newspaper) (MORE)

Because the Clifton Record, way up there in the north, is reporting big changes coming to these parts that'll have a bearing on these travel issues: (reading) "The Pacific Railroad Committee has today voted to consolidate the Missouri, Ft Scott, and Gulf railroads. This new line will run from the Kansas border, all the way south to Galveston, Texas... (some interest) ...connecting up with the Dallas line, which officially opened last month. And will be the first railroad to cross the Indian reservation." (looks up) To us right here folks! And that'll be quite a change. Think y'all agree. (picks up another newspaper) A little federal news now. And it's change there too folks. The Philadelphia Inquirer is reporting on those important negotiations up there in Washington concerning the future of the state of Texas. (reading) "President Grant has ordered the Governor of Texas to accept Articles 13, 14 and 15, of the Constitution, before any return to the Union can be considered. Those articles cover the abolition of slavery; affording former slaves the vote; full repayment of war debts-"
Well I'm sayin' no! I say Texas first and damn them amendments! We let this stand, it ain't gonna stop there. More agreement.
I ain't digging Texas soil to benefit some rich Yankee.
That's right. The soldiers ready for trouble. (stepping in) Suggest y'all watch yourself.

Suggest y'all the same! What you even doin' here? Y'all ain't dealin' with the Indians. Roads. River crossin'... Just beat up on Southern folksHECKLER 3 Told you. Shoulda' kept fightin. That's what I said. For a moment it threatens to boil over.

Alright now, I hear ya. North not helping us and asking a lot in return. But we got a part to play too. There's more than rain and Indians troublin' our roads. I seen it myself with my own eyes. Yesterday, coming outta Wichita. A freighter of colour hanging from a tree.
Kidd looks at the divided room.
I guess these are difficult times.
He turns the page over...
Let's find a different kind of story...
EXT. CHURCH. RED RIVER
The Boudlins running towards the church.
INT. CHURCH. RED RIVER
The reading over. Kidd shaking hands. Mr Boudlin pushes through the crowd.
(reaching Kidd) She's gone.
EXT. CAMP. RED RIVER - NIGHT
Kidd and the Boudlins searching in the rain.
(upset) I was singing her a hymn. Turned my back for a moment, she wasn't there.
She didn't take the horses?
Must have walked out on her own.
(going into the woods) Child?! Are you there?
Mr Bouldin stops Kidd.
(quietly) Look if the kid wants to make her own way so bad. Maybe you should let her. An' go your way too. On Kidd, as,
Will you hurry, Mr Boudlin, please!

EXT. THICKET IN RED RIVER VALLEY - CONTINUOUS Kidd searches through dense woodland. Eventually he reaches a gap in the trees. Ahead: The Red River. Thundering torrents of water. Kidd scans the area. Rain near blinding him. Finally, he sees her. A tiny figure perched high on a rocky outcrop. He shouts. But his voice is drowned out by the water. He moves towards her. A hard climb. Getting closer, he sees: She is shouting across the river. Now she's waving her arms. Kidd follows her line of sight. Sees across the river: A large party of KIOWA INDIANS. She is standing dangerously close to the edge of the outcrop.
(in Kiowa - subtitled shouting) Wait! Wait for me! Don't go. Don't leave me. Come back! The Kiowa keep moving.
(in Kiowa - subtitled) Don't leave me! Don't go!
Kidd moving towards her. Johanna oblivious.
(in Kiowa - subtitled) It's me, daughter of Turning Water and Three Spotted! Come back!
Kidd scrambling up the muddy bank towards her.
Get down! Get down!
(in Kiowa - subtitled) Don't leave me! Don't go!
Finally, Kidd reaches her. Pushes her to the ground to stop her falling. She struggles against him. Looking across the river.
(screaming in Kiowa, subtitled) Don't go! Don't leave me...
But the Kiowa begin to move on. And as they disappear into the distance, she begins to sob desperately. Suddenly just a little girl. Lost and abandoned. Kidd beside her, powerless.
EXT. GENERAL STORE. RED RIVER - NIGHT
Kidd carries a limp Johanna into the store. Puts her on the floor.
She's soaked, poor mite!
Kidd watches as Mrs Boudlin wraps a blanket over her.
She's breathin' at least.
EXT. GENERAL STORE - CONTINUOUS
Kidd walks out onto the porch, wiping mud from his show suit. Mr Boudlin kicking it off his boots.
(takes off his jacket) I mean shit. Will you look at that? (looking at the mud) (MORE)
Sure as I live, that child is trouble. Runnin' like that. I said she had a funny look, didn't I say that?
Mrs Boudlin joins them.
Mr Boudlin, please
Well it's the truth. They sure didn't want her back did they.
They couldn't see she were one of their own.
Well she ain't one of their own! An' she ain't one of us, neither. Child got no clue who she is! Broken pieces is all she's got, and that's a makin' for trouble. No doubt about it.
Kidd looks back at Johanna. Asleep on the shop floor.
I mean shit Kidd, what the hell you gonna do?
We could take her. Raise her ourselves.
Like hell we could! She ain't right in the head! I'm telling you.
We can fix her back. I know we canMR BOUDLIN Doris. We can't go takin' in strange children and callin' them
Why not? You gave me none of my own. This sits there.
Mrs Boudlin starts to cry.
Compose yourself, Doris, please. Mrs Boudlin sobbing now.
We ain't doing it. The girl ain't kin, and that's the last of it.
(finally) I'll take her.
They look at him.
I found her. I'll take her.
Sure about that, Kidd? Castroville's damn near 400 miles.
I've made the journey before.
Them roads have changed since you rode them..
A beat and then,
She's lost. She needs to go home. Maybe find us all a little peace...
EXT. RED RIVER - MORNING
Wide shot of Red River.
INT. GENERAL STORE - MORNING
Johanna, under a blanket. Watching Mrs Boudlin bearing down on her. With a petty-coat, drawers, and a bright yellow dress.
Here child, I got these pretty things from one of my girls...
EXT. GENERAL STORE - SAME TIME
Meanwhile, Kidd and Boudlin in the side alley hitching Kidd's horses to an old wagon.
She ain't been used for a while, but runs pretty good. Appreciate it. (handing him coins) Best I can do right now.
They finish loading stores into the back.
(quietly) You carrying?
(points) Got my twelve-gauge and bird shot. MR BOUDLIN (pulls A REVOLVER from under his jacket) Well I kept this, after Palmito. Figure you need it more than me. (beat) And there's 20 rounds.
Kidd takes the gun and ammo, conceals them in an old flour
I'll fetch it back.
(beat and then) Why you doing this Kidd? What's it to you? Se Kidd. Before he can answer: SCREAMING.

Johanna bursts out the door. The yellow dress half-pulled over her head. Mrs Boudlin close behind. Mrs Boudlin battles to get the dress on. Johanna screaming in Kiowa. Clawing at her violently. Kidd gets up on the wagon. Watches. As Mrs Boudlin finally gets the dress on.
(looking her) By the grace of God child. Don't you look purty.
Johanna looks back at them. Rage inside the ill-fitting dress.
EXT. WAGON. ROAD TO DALLAS - DAY In open country.

The landscape dotted with a handful of OTHER TRAVELLERS. The Red River to their right. Behind them, Red River town in the distance. He catches Johanna looking back across the river. Wiping away a tear. She climbs into the back of the wagon.
(pulling out map) So we'll head to Dallas, that's maybe a week. And then on through the central plains. Cross that and we get to Hill Country...
Hears a CLATTERING behind him. Kidd turns. Johanna reaches for her muddy buckskins.

Course we got readings to do. To pay our way. And we need to keep our eyes open for trouble... We got settlers killing Indians for land. Indians killing them for taking it. Guess you know all about that...
Johanna returns to the front of the wagon. Tries to put the muddy buckskins over the yellow dress. He looks at her. She at him, daring him to challenge her.
Makes no difference to me what you wear..
Kidd puts down the map. Looks at her.
I'm Captain Kidd by the way. (pointing to himself) Captain.
She stares at him blankly.
He points at her - she flinches instinctively.
Johanna. You Johanna.
She glares at him. Turns her back.
Mighty pleased to make your acquaintance, and no mistake...
EXT. CAMP. ROAD TO DALLAS - EVENING A pocket of trees, just off the road.
Johanna rubbing down the horses. Singing softly. Kidd eats, reading his newspapers. Marking up articles.
Well who'd credit that, they've managed to lay a telegraph line from London, England, all the way to India!
He looks up. Watches her at work.
(gestures to his head) Gotta do the head first.
Johanna ignores him. She knows horses. Doesn't appreciate the direction.
Can you even imagine the labour of it? Crossin' oceans, mountains. A tiny little wire, bridging the world like that...
Johanna makes a clicking noise running her hand along the horse's back leg.
(looks up) Happened at birth. Made him cheap. No one wanted him.
She looks at him.
That's Pasha by the way. The other one's Fancy. Not me who named them...
Then starts to lay out a blanket.
You know horses?
Again, she looks at him.
(holds paper up) I'd read you a story, if you could understand it...
She keeps looking. Then turns her back, lies down. Singing quietly. Kidd carefully packs away his newspapers. Checks the revolver. Lies back on his upturned saddle, listening to her singing.
EXT. ROAD TO DALLAS - PRE-DAWN Kidd asleep by the wagon.

He wakes. No sign of Johanna. Kidd gets up. Looks for her. Starting to worry. Then he sees her. A little way away. Watching the horizon. Waiting. The first light of dawn appears above the plains. Beat and then, she turns to him. A moment.
EXT. NORTH TEXAN LANDSCAPE - DAY
Majestic over the landscape. A bird riding the thermals. Johanna, on one of the horses, watches it. She motions to the bird, as though in the presence of the Divine.
(looking up) Chicken hawk. See the tail?
She ignores him. Climbs effortlessly onto a horse. Whispers to it, as the bird wheels away.
And that's a horse.
She turns. Looks at him.
I said it's a horse.
(beat and then) Hors.
See Kidd, she speaks!
Not to be mistaken! Horse. (pats side of wagon) And this is a wagon. Vagorn.
Wagon. Well done.
They ride on a bit further. Then out of the blue,
...Kep-tan.
(surprised) Yes, that's me. Captain. (pointing again) And you're Johanna. Johanna flinches again. KIDD (CONT'D) (still pointing) Johanna.
Kidd puts his finger down.
That's your name. Johanna. She frowns.
And turns away.
EXT. CAMP. EXODUSTER RIVER - EVENING

They camp by a small river. Kidd unloading supplies from the wagon. Johanna crouched, eyeing them with curiosity. We see the revolver, and the shotgun. Kidd lights the stove. Suddenly, the bacon lands next to him. A please would be nice. She looks at him blankly.
You say please. I cook the bacon. Then you say thank you. That's how it's done.
She goes back to his bags. Pulls out a pot of coffee. Tastes it. Spits violently.
(laughing despite himself) Coffee. Packs a punch don't it!
She glares at him, still spitting. Opens a second jar.
Ah, now that's sugar...
She tastes it. Likes it.
Bit better, ain't it.
He hands her a piece of cooked bacon. She shovels sugar on top.
Hey, that ain't cheap! More and more...
(taking the sugar) Yeah, reckon you've had enough of that...
She moves onto Kidd's portfolio. Opens it.
And those are my newspapers. (a little nervous, as she turns through them) Careful now...
She lingers on the printed words.
Words. In a line. (beat) They make a story.
(whispers) Stor-ee.
That's right. Stories.
Johanna takes more newspapers out...
Set down in print and built to last...
She finds the photograph of the dark-haired woman. Holds it
(a beat and then) My wife.. Maria. Kidd gently takes the photograph back.
A moment. Johanna looking at him.
She's down in San Antonio... (beat) Think I'll get us some water.
He goes down to the river.
EXT. ROAD INTO DALLAS - AFTERNOON
As the sun gets low. See DALLAS in the distance. A city being born.
EXT. WAGON. MAIN STREET. DALLAS - AFTERNOON
They move down a busy street. FEDERAL TROOPS on every corner. Johanna hides under a blanket.
EXT. DALLAS LIVERY STABLE - CONTINUOUS

They turn a corner, into a busy Livery Stables. Past BLACKSMITHS and wagon shops. The hammering of metal, and billowing smoke. Johanna watching CROWDS pour off STAGECOACHES and WAGONS. Lines of MIGRANTS drawn by the promise of cheap Texan land. HAWKERS and LAND AGENTS shouting out their rates. More TROOPS watching. Kidd hitches his horses.
(to Johanna) It's safe.
Kidd leads her across the crowded yard. Towards a Telegraph Office.
(to NEWSPAPER SELLER) I'll take Harpers, the Herald.. The London Times if you have it. And the wires.

Behind him, a COMMOTION. Kidd turns. Sees a DEMONSTRATION. A YOUNG PROGRESSIVE SPEAKER on a soap box. Placards reading: Yes to reunion! A GROUP OF TEXAS NO SUPPORTERS jeering. Kidd takes his newspapers and wires and steers Johanna across the yard, towards the rooming house. A SINISTER MAN watches from the shadows. As Kidd and Johanna approach MRS GANNETT (50), the overworked Livery Barn owner.
Hey Kidd... (sees Johanna) She yours?
INT. DALLAS LIVERY STABLE - CONTINUOUS
They move through the crowds.
Stable rooms are full, but I got beds inside. Dollar for the bath
and a pot to fill your belly.
Meal and a hot bath sounds mighty good. And I'll need a room for the girl.
That's a dollar a piece.
(taking out coins) I'm obliged, Mrs Gannett.
Mrs Gannett whistles to STABLE HAND.
(to Stable Hand) See to the horses and wagon here. And side stables need clearing. Don't make me wait on you, you hear?
They start walking again.
(to Kidd) So they paying you to take her home? Or you just taking her out of the goodness of your heart?
They're not paying me. I just know the roads.
They pass PEOPLE spilling out of another WAGON. Carrying tattered belongings.
Look at them. All the way from Lord knows where... Hell-bent on digging up virgin dirt an' calling it home... An' all expecting me to lower my rates! (to GERMAN WORKER) Crates are in. Take 'em to the
kitchen. Die Kuche? Versteht?
GERMAN WORKER
Yeah now! Jetzt, Jetzt!
They enter the Rooming House...
INT. DINING AREA - DALLAS LIVERY STABLES - CONTINUOUS
Into a ramshackle dining area. CROWDED tables. MIGRANTS huddled over maps and contracts. German VOICES, Irish VOICES... All searching for the brave new world of south Texas.
(points to a table, then calls out to KITCHEN SERVER) We got two more here. (looks over) Hey you! You gotta order something if you wanna keep the table.

Mrs Gannett moves off. Kidd and Johanna sit down. Kidd lays out his newspapers and the wire reports. He scans the headlines. Strife and division. Johanna takes in the room. Sees a SOLDIER pass by the window. KITCHEN SERVER puts down two plates of pot food.
(as he reads newspapers) So I reckon stay off federal news tomorrow, what do you think?
Kidd beings eating. Sees Johanna eating with her hands.
We use a knife and fork like this...
Johanna watches him. Then carries on using her hands. Now she's singing.
(embarrassed) And we don't sing. Not in public anyway...
PEOPLE stare at Johanna. Food around her mouth. She stares back at them. Mrs Gannett comes back over. Sees Johanna. Everyone looking.
What y'all looking at? Never seen a child eat before? Mrs Gannett sits.
(to Kidd) What's her name? Johanna.
Pleasure to meet you, Johanna.
She don't speak English. Not a word.
So what does she speak? Kiowa.
Kiowa, huh? (in Kiowa, subtitled) (MORE)
Welcome. (indicates to herself) I am friend.
Johanna stops eating. Looks at her, amazed. Kidd too.
Now you know, that is impressive,
Mrs Gannett.
Got a little Plains. German. Need everything round here. (in Kiowa, subtitled) You are Johanna, yes?
(in Kiowa, subtitled) My name is Cicada. I do not know Cho-hanna.
What's she say?
Says you've been calling her the wrong name. Her name's Cicada.
Kidd looks at Johanna.
Well, she's Johanna now. A beat.
(to Johanna, in English, pointing with her little finger) This man. (in Kiowa, subtitled) He's taking you home.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) I have no home. It was burned by soldiers when I was taken.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Your family?
(in Kiowa, subtitled) All dead.
Outside THREE SOLDIERS walk past the window.
(touches her hair, in Kiowa, subtitled) Because they died?
Johanna nods.
You got salt?

Yeah, I got salt... (to Kitchen Server) Fetch salt out, Josephine. (to Kidd) She says she's got no home. No Kiowa family neither. See the hair? They cut it when they're mourning. Child's an orphan. Twice over.
This lands. Kidd looks at Johanna. She back at him. As though meeting for the first time.
Tell her I'm taking her back to Castroville. To her Aunt and Uncle.
Kidd, she don't have any idea what that means.
It's on her Agency papers. And she's got nowhere else to go.
(to Johanna, in Kiowa) He's taking you to another home.
Johanna impassive. Starts eating again.
(to Kidd) Hear them roads are bad down Castroville way. Word is Billy Yanks ain't patrolling them no
That's what I hear...
Mr Gannett used to travel that way. Before he went to California.
Another group of MIGRANTS enter.
What about that salt?
(to the Customer) It's coming alright!
How long's he been gone?
Two years. (beat) Or maybe he just didn't wanna come back.
And she leaves.
INT. CORRIDOR - CONTINUOUS
Mrs Gannett closes the door. They walk down the corridor.
You know, I don't have the first clue of caring for a child. Never had the need. Or the patience required.
She's still alive ain't she? That's not nothing.
Reach the room next door. Kidd enters. Another lonely bedroom. He turns to see Mrs Gannett at the door.
INT. KIDD'S ROOM. DALLAS LIVERY STABLES - NIGHT
Kidd sat at the window. Studying his newspapers and wires. Mrs Gannett behind him. In bed.
Road taking its toll?
Sleeping's not what it was.
Stories only keep you company so long...
He watches steam rising up from the horses down in the Livery.
So what you gonna do? When you've taken her?
Thinking I might keep going south. Work a passage out of Galveston. See one of those far off places I read about every night.
She gets up. Pulls on some clothes.
This ain't where you're supposed to be, Kidd. Running, hiding. (beat) And Castroville's San Antonio way. Kidd knows where this is going.
(beat) How long's it been?
Coming on five years.
Reckon you need to go back. Make it right with her.
Don't know if I can.
She goes to the door. Turns to Kidd.
Road you're on, seems like you got no choice. (beat) But I'm thinking you already knew that.
Mrs Gannett leaves Kidd alone in the dark.
EXT. STREET. NEAR MASONIC HALL. DALLAS - DAY

Kidd hammers up a poster outside the Masonic Hall. He walks on. Johanna follows. Aware he's not himself. They reach a Dime Store. Johanna attracted to the window display. Bric-a-brac, childish things. Meanwhile across the street, another DEMONSTRATION. Placards. Flags. More SCUFFLES. FEDERAL SOLDIERS pull them apart. Johanna's eyes settle on several CORN DOLLS. See Johanna; this means something. A distant memory. But unclear... Kidd turns away from the demonstration. Sees her looking. Across the street, that SINISTER MAN again. They walk on.
EXT. HALL. DALLAS - EVENING
INT. BACK STAGE AREA - MASONIC HALL - CONTINUOUS

Kidd putting on his show suit. Next to him, his newspapers unread. Johanna looks back at him, from the doorway. Knows he's troubled. She watches the hall filling. PEOPLE putting dimes into the tin pot. TROOPS watching. The Sinister Man enters. TWO OTHER MEN beside him. Throw their dimes in... Meanwhile, Kidd stands alone, at a shard of mirror. Haunted by the past. A tug at his side. Johanna, holding out his portfolio. A wordless apology. Kidd looks down at her. And then he walks out onto the stage.
He turns the pages. The room waits.

...But it's hard. War's over five years but you wouldn't know for the look of these pages. (struggling now) Makes me wonder what the point of even reading the news is... See his audience. This isn't what they paid for.
The Sinister Man watching intently.

(at last) Alright now. Here's one... Page three of the London Times, February 19th: 'Miracle on the High Seas' Now two months ago, our correspondent reported the polar exploration ship, the Hansa, sunk in unchartered waters, north of the Arctic Circle. Its crew of 39 souls lost forever... (beat) Well, I have news for y'all yet. (begins reading) "A Russian whaling ship, on course for St. Petersburg, claims to have spotted the Hansa afloat. The Captain.. (checks paper) Name of Morozov... Sent word by telegraph that he had made contact with the crew who by the grace of God were still alive. But sadly, in the heavy seas and fog common to those parts, he lost sight of the Hansa in the night. And by dawn she was gone again. See the audience. A story at last...
But wait on this... According to this latest report, a naval vessel has been dispatched from England. The HMS Warrior no less.. (MORE)

And as we sit here tonight she is steaming hard for the Hansa's last reported position. While back home, the families of those lost boys wait and pray for salvation. For, in the words of the Warrior's commanding officer, one Captain
(reading again) "Those boys must be found," he signals, "Theirs is the worst fate a man can face. For to be lost, is to cease to exist at all..."
See Kidd. This lands with him.
Is that it?
That's all I have. The most recent report.
He turns to another story.
How about a little fashion news...
EXT. STREET. DALLAS - NIGHT
Kidd and Johanna leave the Masonic Hall. Johanna sees a detachment of FEDERAL SOLDIERS across the street. Moves a little closer to Kidd. Suddenly, a VOICE in the dark.
A word, Captain?
Kidd turns.
Almay falls in step.
Enjoyed your reading, Captain. You take us away from our hard thoughts. (beat) And it's good to make acquaintance of a fellow military man. Where'd you serve, Captain? Galveston.
1st Texas Infantry. North Virginia.
Well alright, an' how can I assist you, Mr Almay?

I'll get to that, Cap... But let me ask you this. Don't it just rile you them callin' us defeated? It's like you said, the war ain't over. They might'av taken the good out my slinging arm and half my men with it, but that don't mean it's done. Way I see it, we fought but ain't no piece of this ours. No piece at all. You know what I'm saying?
It's late, Mr Almay, what's your point?
The point is. Us old soldiers gotta live, right? So I've a little business proposition for you. Seein' you travellin' alone like you are with this young girl.
Kidd stops.
What do you want?
How much? For the girl? She's not for sale.
Kidd starts walking again.
Word is she's that captive out of Wichita.
You're well informed.
News of value travels. (beat and then) How about $50? Save you the trip down to Castroville. You know Billy Yanks ain't patrolling southern roads no more... No place for an old man and a child.
Kidd takes Johanna by the hand.
$100 then. And you can rest easy knowing at least she'll get paid. I mean, look at that fair skin. Bet you were a lucky man out there in the desert...
Suddenly, Kidd grabs Almay. Pushes him hard against a wall.
(cooly, to Kidd) In the alternative. We could just take her...
A small patrol of FEDERAL SOLDIERS approach.
What's going on?
(to the Soldiers) Thank the Lord. We're local traders, Sir. We became concerned for the welfare of this here child. In the clutches of a strange man such as this.
I'm transporting this child to her relatives in the south. I have the official documentation here.
He hands the Federal Officer the Indian Agency papers.
The soldiers search Almay and company. Find their guns.
God damnit. (to Kidd) You get on your way.
A look between Almay and Kidd, as he's taken away.
EXT/INT. LIVERY STABLES - NIGHT
Kidd and Johanna moving fast. They turn a corner into the stables.
EXT. WAGON. ROAD OUT OF DALLAS - NIGHT
Kidd steers the wagon down narrow streets.
EXT. COMMAND POST. DALLAS - NIGHT
Almay and crew led to a Command Post.
EXT. WAGON. ROAD OUT OF DALLAS - NIGHT
They turn onto a main road, heading out of town. Kidd riding hard.
EXT. COMMAND POST. DALLAS - NIGHT
Almay and his crew waiting. A Federal Officer in no hurry to deal with them.
EXT. WAGON. CENTRAL PLAINS - NIGHT
Kidd riding hard. Sounds of wheel clicking. Johanna looking nervously behind.
EXT. COMMAND POST. DALLAS - NIGHT
Almay and his crew still waiting.
EXT. COMMAND POST. DALLAS - NIGHT
Almay and his crew leaving the command post. See Almay. Only one thing on his mind.
EXT. LIVERY STABLES. NIGHT
Almay and men saddle up. Almay opens his saddle pack. More GUNS. They head out in pursuit.
EXT. WAGON. CENTRAL PLAINS - NEAR DAWN
The first light of dawn. Kidd sees ahead: A ridge of hills in the distance. Suddenly, Kep-tan!
Kidd turns. And sees in the distance...
EXT. ALMAY'S CREW. CENTRAL PLAINS - CONTINUOUS EXT. WAGON. CENTRAL PLAINS - CONTINUOUS
Kidd reaches behind for the shotgun.
She passes him the flour keg. Kidd pulls out the revolver and ammo. Loads it. Looks behind again. Three horses. Definitely them.
EXT. ALMAY'S CREW. CENTRAL PLAINS - CONTINUOUS
Almay whipping his horse. His men too.
EXT. WAGON. CENTRAL PLAINS - CONTINUOUS
Kidd looking for cover. Ahead: a left turn which runs into the hills.
He looks back...
EXT. ALMAY'S CREW. CENTRAL PLAINS - CONTINUOUS
Almay and crew gaining ground...
EXT. WAGON. RAVINE - DAY
Kidd enters the hills. Scans the slopes either side. Looking for a place to hide. Sees another smaller track off to the left. Takes it.
EXT. WAGON. RAVINE TRACK - CONTINUOUS

Kidd drives the wagon up the small track. The horses struggling. Kidd wills them on. His shoulder an agony. Horses exhausted. Suddenly, a CRUNCH... One of the wheels cracks. Kidd leaps out and drags the horses forward. Kidd straining every muscle. Johanna, clinging to the side of the wagon. Finally, the track levels out. A place to hide. Kidd quickly unharnesses the horses. Crawls behind a low rock. Peers down at the road below. His POV: Almay approaching the fork.
EXT. CROSSROADS - CONTINUOUS
Almay and his crew reach the fork. Almay looks up at the ridges, trying to work out which way...
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS

Kidd sees them take the left fork. Shit. He signals to Johanna to stay down. Moves to another position. To get a better view below. Nothing. Meanwhile, Johanna rips the bottom of her skirt. Ties the rag around her head. Rummages in Kidd's bag. Finds his knife. Fixes it to her waist. They wait. Only the sound of wind. Suddenly: a gun-shot slams into the side of the wagon. The horses bolt. Johanna slides to the ground. Kidd fires back in the direction of the shot. Then crawls to the wagon, as more shots slam into the dirt nearby.
He leads Johanna to a new position. Kidd stops. Looks. Nothing.
EXT. ALMAY'S POSITION. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Almay looks up the ravine. Calculating Kidd's location. He spots the abandoned wagon. Signals wordlessly to his men. They split up. Advance up the ravine in a pincer attack...
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Kidd sees one of Almay's men moving up the ravine from the left. He fires. But there's immediate return fire. Slamming into the rocks around them. Kidd struck in the face with rock fragments.
And they move. Blood pouring down Kidd's face.
EXT. ALMAY'S POSITION. HIGH BLUFF
Almay, still climbing.
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
As they scramble towards cover, Kidd sees another of Almay's men down to the right. Aims. Fires... But then has incoming from the left. They make it to cover.
EXT. ALMAY'S POSITION. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Almay looks up.
He waves his men on.
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Kidd scans the ravine. Looking for a way out.
Johanna sees a branch on the ground. A large rock.
She works it under the rock. Strains to lever it free.

As suddenly, the rock tumbles down the slope... Driving Almay's left flank man out into the open. Kidd fires. Brings him down. Almay and right flank man fire back. Kidd and Johanna forced to retreat again.
They reach cover. Kidd looking for a way out. Sees one of his horses, off to the side.
(points) Johanna. Horse. Kidd fires off more rounds.
(urgently) I shoot. Johanna go. Kep-tan.
No. You go. More shots incoming. They're getting closer now.
What you say, Cap? Talk to me. Kidd fires back.
(insistent)
(points at the shotgun) Kep-tan. Useless. Only good for birds.
He rummages in his bag. Opens one of the shell casings to show her the bird shot.
See?! Useless. No good. (points to horse) You have to go!

More shots. They hug the rock. Johanna looks at the casings. Kidd reloads. Not many rounds left. Fires another brace. A moment between them. And then Johanna runs. Kidd watches her go. Fires again to cover her.
(buying time) Well you certainly got me in a difficult situation, Mr Almay. So tell me how it would work.
Almay signals his flanker to close in.
I figure a share for each man and an extra one for me...
Johanna running. Past the horse. To the wagon. Pulls out the
Meanwhile, Kidd sees he only has one bullet left. Knows they're closing in for the kill.
(still playing for time) That seems pretty fair to me. So how we gonna do this?
Suddenly, Johanna appears. Throws down the bag of dimes.
(frustrated) Damn it Johanna. Bribing's not gonna work.
Just gotta hand over the girl. That's all.
Johanna takes the empty shell casing. Hurriedly loads it with dimes. Hands it to Kidd. Finally he understands. A moment between them. Kidd loads a useless bird shot shell. Stay down.
He crawls forward. As Johanna starts filling another casing with dimes. And Kidd takes a bead on Almay's position. Fires...
EXT. ALMAY'S POSITION. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
...The shot sprays weakly against a rock.
Oh Cap, what you trying to do, now? Tickle us to death?
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Kidd loads another.
Another feeble bird shot scatters.
EXT. ALMAY'S POSITION. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Well you're embarrasin' yourself. (beat) Then again, maybe you only had twenty rounds for the handgun. And all you got left is birdshot.
He waits. Silence.
Almay signs to his flanker and starts to advance...
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Kidd sees Almay and the flanker coming towards them. Less concerned about cover.
(confused) Captain, boom?
He loads one of Johanna's shells.
He throws his revolver out into the dirt.
EXT. ALMAY'S POSITION. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS
Almay sees Kidd's revolver out in the open. He nods silently to his man, JONES.
Almay motions to Jones to stand up. Smiling. Jones stands.
EXT. HIGH BLUFF - CONTINUOUS

Kidd stands too. But then Jones goes for his gun. Suddenly, Kidd pulls out the shot-gun. The gun roars. Sending Kidd violently backwards. As the shell slams into the Jones' chest. His body tumbles down. See Almay. He wasn't expecting that.
opposite sides again.
Guess that damn war's keepin' time for us all the way. Got us partnered and we got no choice but to fall in step. (beat) No choice at all.

Almay quickly moves left to cut Kidd off. Kidd sees the move. Moves himself. And now we're cat-and-mouse amongst the rocks. Mano e mano under a Texan sun. Until... Kidd behind a rock. Johanna beside him. Hands him another loaded shell. He inches around. Looking for Almay... Everything suddenly quiet. Just the body of Jones lying out in the open. The other down the slope. On Kidd. Did Almay run? Suddenly, Almay appears on Kidd's blind side... He fires. But Kidd goes low and fires too. Hits Almay square in the chest.
A moment. Kidd surveys the battle field, blood running from his wound. The bodies lying there. He retrieves his revolver. Only one bullet left.
EXT. RAVINE ROAD - DAY
Kidd pulls them up another hill. Wagon shot to pieces. The wheel clanking. The reach a secluded spot.
EXT. CAMP - NIGHT

Kidd patching his wound, watching the plain below. Johanna tending the horses. She spits into the red earth. Puts her finger in the paste. Starts painting shapes on the horse's flank, as she sings. Kidd knows what this is. The story of the battle. She looks back at him. Trying to read his expression.
EXT. CAMP - LATER THAT NIGHT Johanna asleep.
Kidd throws his coat over her. Sit hunched against his saddle, the campfire throwing shadows across his beaten-up face. The longer the road, the harder it is.
EXT. TEXAS PRAIRIE - DAWN
Sun up over the immense Texas prairie.
EXT. WAGON. ON THE ROAD SOUTH - MORNING

They ride on. In the distance: Buffalo. But noticeably thinner than before. Joanna watching him. She tentatively touches her face. Pointing with her lips and little finger. Kidd realises. Touches the wound on his face.
Kidd stretches back. Easing the tension in his shoulder.
We're OK. Just keep to the road. We'll get there.
Johanna leans down. Picks up a dime piece from the floor of the wagon. Turns it in her hand. Makes it reflect in the sun.
(seeing this) That's a dime. Dime-ah...
That's right. It's money. Boom!
Yeah, I guess it can be both.
She takes a strip of leather from her buckskins. Fashions the
(sees it) Pretty...

He looks at her. She smiles. Suddenly, the wagon lurches. Kidd almost comes out of his seat. Johanna stifles a laugh. Kidd notices. A beat and then, He lurches the cart the other way. Deliberately. Pretends to unseat. And now she belly laughs. Kidd lurches again. Nearly falling off for real this time. And now he's belly laughing too. Both surprised at the other.
EXT. WAGON. ON THE ROAD SOUTH - LATER THAT DAY
They ride on. Johanna quietly singing a lullaby. A loaded FREIGHT WAGON rolls past in the opposite direction. Kidd listening. She looks up. Birds dancing high above them. Bird. We call that a bird.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Bird.
She looks at him. Repeats. Expecting him to copy her.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Bird. See Kidd. This is something new. (repeating her Kiowa) Bird.
She mocks his pronunciation. Kidd tries again. Now she's laughing. She points to a tree.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Tree. (in Kiowa) Tree. (knows he's being mocked) Alright, cos you're so smart...
He looks out. Sees a cactus.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Sage!
She opens her talisman. Breathes in.
Smells good don't it. Especially when you burn it. (in Kiowa, subtitled) Sage.
(in English) Goot...
A moment of understand between them. They can communicate.
She points out at the prairie with her lips.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Earth. (in Kiowa, subtitled) Earth. (in English) What's that?
(in Kiowa, her palm flat as if stroking) Earth. (trying to understand) Flat? Dirt? Earth? Earth. (in Kiowa, subtitled) Earth. (in English) The Earth, I get it.
Then she points up at the sky. Sky.
(in Kiowa, subtitled)
Kidd watching.
He watches as Johanna wraps her arms around herself.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Earth. Sky. Tree. Birds. Daw. I don't understand. Daw. What's
She motions again. And exhales gently.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Wind? Breath?
She brings her arms together again.
She blows again. Her arms wrapped around her whole world.
Then she makes a sign. A circle.
He draws a line with his finger.
Where I come from, it's more of a line.
She watches him draw it.
And that's how we live. Until, once in a while, when the sun gets low and the soft wind blows, we wonder how the hell we got here...
He trails off. She makes the sign of a line.
That's right, a line. Very good.
Velly goot.
Kidd laughs.
Velly goot indeed!
(laughing) Velly good, Onkle!
See Kidd. He turns to face her.
Onkle? Did you say Onkle? Onkle...
Kidd pulls the wagon to a stop.
She looks over at him, as surprised as he is.
Can you remember anything else?
A moment as she thinks. And then:
Ist du meine Onkle?
No, I'm not your uncle.
She frowns. Struggling with a new memory.
What? What is it?

She looks at him. As though for help. Then slowly a look of horror crosses her face. As if fearful memories lie deep inside. And are pushing up to the surface. Kidd sees her struggling. Confusion. And rising terror.
(knows what this is) Hey, leave it. Forget it. I shouldn't have asked.
And he rides on.
Let's do animals?
He looks out. Spots a jack-rabbit.
See? Jack-rabbit. What's rabbit?
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Rabbit.
(in Kiowa) Rabbit. (in English) Oh that's a tricky one. Say it again.
But Johanna has noticed something ahead.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Buffalo... A mutilated buffalo carcass. By the side of the track. A bit further on:
More skinned carcasses lying out under the hot sun. The sound of insects. A baby buffalo standing helpless beside its dead mother.
(in Kiowa, angry) How pitiable.. Terrible...
Kidd looks ahead: Forest.
EXT. ON THE ROAD SOUTH - DAY

Kidd and Johanna, moving through the trees. A sense of menace. Ahead a bend... They round it and see... A GROUP OF MEN on the road. Kidd slows the wagon. OTHER ARMED MEN appear through the trees. Guns. Knives. Confederate uniforms. Native American elements. Menace.
Gentlemen... BAD ASS Roads closed. Nobody unaccounted for is getting into Erath County.
That the law? It is now.
BAD ASS
They start searching the wagon.
Got nothing of great value back there.
MORE MEN appear. One with the look of THE LEADER. His name is FARLEY.
What's your name, sir? Captain Kidd.
An' your reason for travellin', Captain?
I read the news.
One of the men finds Kidd's newspapers. Hands them to the Farley. Meanwhile, Bad Ass begins slowly circling the wagon. Eyes the fresh bullet holes.
Carrying some damage back here.
Got the wagon cheap. She came with the holes.
(reading) Well you sure got holes in your newspapers, Mister.
Read just fine to me.
Well ain't nothing here about Erath County. Hell of a lot's been happening but I don't see it here...
Yeah, we been busy, we dawn fixed them Mexicans. Indians too. Pushed
them clean out. Way beyond Leon
Mr Farley, he killed a right smart of Indians. Scalped 'em good too.
We're building a whole new world of opportunity down in Erath County. But ain't none of it writ here, newsman.
Guess that news didn't travel. Beat and then,
Farley, gestures. BAD ASS, lifts his gun. Move..
BAD ASS
Kidd knows he has no choice.
EXT. OUTSKIRTS OF DURAND TOWN - AFTERNOON
Kidd and Johanna are led towards an abandoned military camp on the outskirts of a town.
EXT. ABANDONED MILITARY CAMP. OUTSIDE DURAND TOWN - EVENING97

To one side: CHINESE AND AFRICAN-AMERICAN LABOURERS huddle under rotting canvas. Smoke in the air. To the other side: POOR WHITES. Buffalo carcasses on frames. ARMED MEN on horseback watching. FARLEY leads them to an empty spot.
(to Bad Ass) Ten minutes. Then bring 'em into town.
What exactly do you have in mind, Mr Farley?
We're gonna have us a little reading.
He rides off, leaving two men guarding: BAD ASS. And a YOUTH by the name of JOHN CALLEY. Kidd unhitches the horses.
(to Bad Ass) Just feeding the horses...
Johanna takes the horses, whispering to them.
She wrong in the head?
Kidd pulls his show suit down from the wagon.
Cos our baby cousin gawn wrong in the head, said it cos her mama don drink, but I'm wonderin' some are jus born that way.
That right...
That's right.
Kidd starts to dress.
So they pay you to tell stories?
I read the news.
That right? (clearly an unfamiliar concept) I don't read none, anyways.
So what do you do?
Whatever Mr Farley be needing. And what business is Mr Farley in?
(interrupting) Business of never mind your business.
Johanna leads the horses back.
So this Mr Farley he family to you?

Oh Mr Farley aint kin of mine, I ain't got no kin now Tommy's gone, but work for Mr Farley all same... Like a family I guess, can't go leaving, like a family... Thought of it, after Tommy, but Mr Farley's plained he didn't got no choice what happened and Tommy seen it coming and should have watched himself, and I seen that, I seen that.
Kidd putting his collar into place.
And what happened to Tommy?
Mr Farley gawn shot him, for hollarin, questionin... Got thoughts of things in his head, couldn't keep 'em in there.
Johanna hands Kidd his newspapers. Points at the flour keg gun box...
(quietly) Dime-ah? He shakes his head. No.
Working for Farley can't be easy then...

Don't know easy, but I'm sayin' Tommy seen it coming, and he weren't no easy guy that's the truth, specially when he was in drink. Mr Farley right about that. Tommy only my brother but he gawn near raised me... But Mr Farley is knowing how things are, as they needs to be, and he been real kind to me, taken me in after Tommy and he don't need to have done that, and I'm grateful. I'm grateful... (whistles) Well I sure am waitin' on hearin' them news readings Mister, we ain't had no show round here for few years now. Not since military folk left... Say maybe you'll be doing more than one reading...
We're leaving in the morning. Sadly. Other places to be. See Calley. A flash of disappointment.
Road's calling, I seen that. Bad Ass waves his gun.
Time to go.
EXT. DURAND TOWN - NIGHT

Kidd and Johanna led towards town. Past railroad box cars and freight wagons being stacked with buffalo hides. Nearby, more buffalo carcasses. A mountain of buffalo heads. To one side, a huge PYRE. Flames leaping into the air.
INT. DURAND TOWN SQUARE - NIGHT
Like a mining town, CROWDED, chaotic. In one corner, a saloon. Out front, Farley. Surrounded by his men.
(sees Kidd) Well don't you look mighty fine, Captain?
Where do you want me?
(drinks) Them's your audience...
Kidd looks at the crowd. Drunk. Oblivious. He leads Johanna to an open barn. Lays out his newspapers on a bench. Johanna picks up the dime pot. Takes it defiantly out into the crowd... DIME-AH!
Before Kidd can react, Bad Ass hands him a newspaper.
Mr Farley says to read this.

Kidd looks at it: The Erath Journal. On the front page, an etching of Farley, surrounded by buffalo heads. A bunch of Native American scalps in his hand. Meanwhile across the square, Johanna reaches Farley.
(holds out the pot) DIME-AH!
Farley looks right across at Kidd. A moment between them. He drops it in the pot.
My name's Captain Kidd and, Mr Farley has asked me to come here tonight, to read y'all the news... More interest.

Now I always start my readings with something local. And Mr Farley has kindly given me this copy of the Erath Journal which I must confess I'm not familiar with. (he turns the pages. More Farley etchings, more scalps) Sure looks like Mr Farley is a busy man in these parts. A businessman. A law giver. There's the buffalo, a profitable business for sure... The timber, the clearing of the plains... And all of you fine folk working for him at that. Sun-up to sun-down.. The way I see it, none of that is news. Isn't that right Mr Farley?
He looks across at Farley.

So let me see if I can't tempt you with something else. Harper's Illustrated have a story.. (retrieves it) Reporting from the lonely little town of Keel Run, Pennsylvania. Now Keel Run ain't known for much - I'm counting none of y'all here has heard of it. And why should you? It's in the North for starters... (few grins) (MORE)
Just one of a thousand towns 'cross our nation, birthed by the work of many but enjoyed by the few...
See Farley. Where's this going?

Now Keel Run don't trade in buffalo, but in coal. But just like you, every morning, its men rise early from their beds only to descend into the great mine that runs like a labyrinth beneath their homes... (more people listening) "On the morning of February 11th, thirty men of the Run" did just as this and come noon, everything was as it should be and always had been... Hard, hard labouring... But before the next hour was up, Keel Run's wheel of fortune had turned. For the mine... was on fire.
See his audience hooked. Farley watching intently. Johanna too. Watching the impact of Kidd's story on the

Now according to Harper's here, "The fire started in the eastern shaft and spread fast back through the mine carried by the pull of air working its way to the surface. Building and building. Into a raging inferno." A beast devouring everything that dare stand in its way. (beat) Now you and I can only imagine the fear those thirty men felt. The first dozen died within minutes. Another seven not long after. (beat) But I'm not here to tell y'all the story of those unfortunate souls. Or of the mine owner who'd been so lax about their safety. Sitting up there in his fancy home... No I'm here to tell you about the eleven men who lived. The eleven men who fought back to escape their fate.
Thought I told you to read from the Erath, Captain.
Well see, I was wondering if folks might prefer some storytelling from places outside of Erath. Just for tonight.
Think you should read the Erath all the same, Captain. Sorta thing these people expect to hear.
Sure seems a shame to miss the opportunity, Mr Farley... (turns to the crowd) How's about we vote on it? I can read this here Journal. Or I can keep on with the story of Keel Run. A beat, then:
I'd 'preciate hearin' about the men of that Run.
Agreement in the audience. Voices growing in confidence.
Stor-ee! Stor-ee!

Well all right then... (sees Farley talking to Bad Ass) That day those eleven men were facing a mortal enemy. Intent on destroying everything they ever cared about. Everything they'd built... (sees Farley's men move into the crowd) Every pillar of progress and civilization...

(roughly pushing) Show's over folks (faster now) Well I'll tell ya, those men refused defeat. In the dark they kept their heads. Worked together. Fought back against the odds. For better lives. And for freedom.
PEOPLE begin to protest. Scuffles.
Get on out!
I'll keep reading folks. Long as you want me to.

And then it kicks off. Farley's men against townsfolk. Townsfolk against Farley's men. Kidd sees Johanna out in the crowd. Kicking one of Farley's men. Then she's knocked over. The dime pot spilling in the dust. He pushes through towards her. Grabs her hand just in time.
EXT. DURAND STREET - NIGHT
Kidd and Johanna walking fast. Behind them the sound of fighting. Gunshots. Ahead, Farley GOONS coming back into town to quell the trouble, some on horse back. Kidd avoids them.
EXT. DURAND. NEAR THE RAILROAD CARS. NIGHT

Kidd and Johanna approach the railroad cars. Kidd sees ahead; more Farley GOONS. He pulls Johanna into a railroad car. They press themselves into the shadows as the GOONS pass. They stumble through the railroad car, buffalo carcasses either side. Then climb down and make their way towards camp.
EXT. DURAND CAMP - NIGHT They reach the camp.

Johanna climbs into the wagon. As Kidd hitches the horses. Kidd sees the damaged wheel. Looks bad. No time to fix it. Suddenly, the sound of a gun being cocked. Kidd turns to see Farley's gun trained on him. Next to him, Bad Ass.
You shoulda just read, Captain.
Just giving them a choice, Mr Farley.
Farley throws down the copy of the Erath Journal.
Well you can read now. (beat) Seems like you need the education.
I travel plenty. Just figure this is my land too.
Then y'all know it's disputed. And this here is the frontline. We gotta deal with all what threatens us. Mexicans, Reds, Blacks, Yanks, don't matter, all the same.
Back in the wagon, Johanna opens the gun box.
We gotta stop fighting some time.
We will. When it's ours alone.

Johanna carefully pulls out the revolver. Farley motions to Bad Ass, who grabs Kidd. And Farley punches him square in the guts. As Johanna opens the ammo box. One round left. Farley punches Kidd again. Kidd falls to the ground. Farley kicks him. Kidd on his knees.
You ready to read, Captain?
(spitting blood) Killing me won't get you heard.

Farley lifts the gun. Kidd stares Farley down. He's about to pull the trigger, when... BANG! Farley staggers backwards. Kidd looks over. Johanna. Gun raised. She turns the gun on Bad Ass. CLICK. Bad Ass realises. She's out. Slowly pulls up his gun.
And Bad Ass drops too. Kidd wheels around... John Calley in the shadows. Gun in hand. Watches Farley, coughing blood. Moving towards his gun... BANG! Calley shoots him dead.
(holds out Kidd's newspapers) I like your stories. You need keep tellin' them. See Kidd.
He goes to Johanna. She's looking at the slumped bodies. He carefully takes the gun.
(then to Calley) Get in!
A handful of LABOURERS in the shadows, watching them leave.
EXT. WAGON - CONTINUOUS
Kidd driving fast. Calley gun poised, watching the darkness behind. Kidd looks back at Johanna. She at him. A moment. Kidd knows he's brought killing back to her door.
EXT. WAGON. CENTRAL PLAINS - DAY
Still on the move. Slower now. Calley sits next to Kidd cleaning his gun. The SOUND of that broken wheel. Kidd glances back at Johanna. She's looking out at the wide plains.
I ain't never heard of news reading as a thing a man could do.
It's no rich man's occupation that's for sure. Not in Texas anyways...
So why'd you bother?
That's a long story.
Seems like we got all day.
Well, I was a printer by trade. Before. Had a works. Down in San Antonio. Hot metal and black ink it's a beautiful thing to see what they can make.
Kidd pauses, his expression shifting.
One night this story came in for printing. Saying a Virginian man a businessman of sorts - had arrived in Mississippi, intent on raising hell among the slaves down there. Of arming them, no less.
Sounds 'bout right for them damn Yankees.
Yeah well, I printed it. And it stirred up a hell of its own.

People up and raging about it. (beat) Couple of days later, more news came. This Virginian had been caught. Dragged from a coach.. And beaten to death. (beat) Only it turned out it wasn't true. He was just down there trying to sell some shoes. That's all. Just shoes. (beat) Well I saw them lies come thick and fast after that. From both sides. Printed more than my fair share. And War followed as I knew it would.
Kidd looks down at the wheel.

After the killing was over and I came home. There was no print works. I lost everything. Same as everyone. (beat) But print was in my blood I guess. Eventually I got to thinking... Maybe I should do my own readings. Maybe if we had the truth of things laid out clearly we'd be less inclined to pick up our guns again... (beat) That was the idea anyway.
Well, way I now it, don't look like that's been working so well.
Guess not...
Kidd looks back at Johanna. She meets his look. Knows. Calley sees. Puts down his gun.
Then again, maybe I just like stories.
(nodding to Johanna) She kin?
No. She was orphaned by the Kiowa down in Hill Country. They took her North. I'm taking her back to her living relatives.
So why you the one takin' her?
Something I can do I guess. See Calley, thinking on this.
I'm reckonin' we killed some Kiowa recent. All sorta look the same though. I'll tell ya they were mad as hell for us shootin' up them cows.
They need the buffalo to survive.
Guessin' that's why Mr Farley liked killin' em. It's sure good business. Dollar for a head at least!
This sits there. Calley embarrassed.
So what you gonna do now?
Find some place where there's work. Tommy always talked 'bout fixin' on the railroad...
Well I'd say Tommy had it right. Gonna be a lot more railroads coming. (beat) You should go. Make something of your own. See Calley, considering this new future.
Well how 'bout that?
Kidd looks down at that tapping wheel again.
So how about you? What'u gonna do, when you fetched her back? Oh I don't know.
You got family and all?
I got a wife. Down in San Antonio.
(laughing) Well she sure must be the merciful type! I'm thinkin' you need to go down there an show her a little 'ppreciation.
Maybe I should at that.
EXT. PLAINS WATER HOLE - NIGHT

A hard wind. A cluster of WAGONS camped. Calley by the fire. Johanna lying under a blanket. Kidd kneeling beside the wagon. Fixing the damaged wheel. Kidd straightens up. Looks across the dark plains... A distant flash of lightning. He looks over at Johanna. She's looking over at a line of large white Conestoga wagons. Their canvases moving in the wind. Slap, slap, slap... Kidd sees those distant memories cross her face again, like clouds on the plain, turning the prairie grass black.
You want some water? (beat) Johanna?
She turns away from him.
You sure she ain't gone in the head?
EXT. PLAINS - DAY
They approach a fork in the road. One road points north. A LINE OF WAGONS preparing to take it. The other points south. To Hill Country, visible in the far distance.
(pulling up) This is you, Calley.
Calley gets out. Lingers.
I could come with. I seen what it's like, Hill Country... An you've seen me shoot.
Railroads due north. I guess...
Johanna looks across at the wagons. The canvas flapping in the wind. Sees the faces in the back: WOMEN, CHILDREN. Faces of hardship. Wonder. Fear. Again, we see something stirring in her.
Least ways you could wait on a wagon train - stead of ridin' alone?
Don't seem to be many heading that way.
Calley takes out his gun. Empties the bullets into his palm. Holds them out.
Take 'em for her, at least.
A beat. Kidd takes the bullets.
(beat) Say Cap, those men, holed up in that mine. They really beat that fire and get home?
They really did, Calley. (leans down, hands him the copy of Harper's Illustrated) See, it's printed right there in black and white.
(looks down at it) Well Goddamn, ain't that something.
And he walks away. To the wagons taking the road North. Kidd turns back. The looming hills ahead. And Johanna, waiting for him.
No, dime'ah. And they move off.
EXT. WAGON. HILL COUNTRY - DAY

Steep valleys. High plateaus. Sharp ravines. Markedly different from any place they've been. Kidd alert to danger. Sees a string of caves in the bluffs high above. Is there movement up there? Johanna sees the caves too. She knows what they are... Kidd pulls the gun towards him.
Let's practice that song, shall we?
She looks over at him.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) The song? (in English) Can you teach me? (starts to sing) Wa ho wa ho wa ho...
She joins in. Helps him. The damaged wheel groans under the strain.
What does it mean then? (in Kiowa, subtitled) Rabbit...
(in English) Rabbit.
She starts to sing again as he cracks the reins.
EXT. BLUFF. HILL COUNTRY - SAME TIME
High POV, looking down at the lonely wagon below, as if observed.
EXT. CAMP. HILL COUNTRY - NIGHT
They camp. Kidd watching the shadows. A coyote CRIES. Something disturbs the horses. Their ears up. Kidd pushes dirt over the flames. The gun close.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) The land is angry.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) We'll start early.
EXT. ROAD. HILL COUNTRY - DAY Back on the road.
Johanna looking up at a huge rock formation to her right. Something familiar about the shape of it as they pass.
(sees her looking) What? What is it? You've seen this before?
She doesn't answer. He drives the horses on. Out in the plains, Kidd sees a reflection.
EXT. ROAD. HILL COUNTRY - DUSK
Kidd still riding. Up hill now. Beside him, Johanna watching the valley below. Familiar. Kidd, scans the horizon. Dust clouds.
EXT. WOODLAND. HILL COUNTRY - DAY

They enter woodland. Ancient, distinctive trees. Dense shadows. They turn a corner. A tree lying across the track. Kidd slows. Scans the area. It could be a trap... He parks up. Climbs out the wagon. Revolver out. Walks over to the tree. Back in the wagon, Johanna takes out Kidd's knife. Watches Kidd as he begins moving the trunk. She gets out to help. Get back! Beat on Johanna. She looks down the road ahead of them... Then to the left at the distinctive shapes of the ancient oaks...
(in Kiowa) Wagon! Now!! Finally, she complies.
Kidd clears a path. Gets back in the wagon. Drives the horses on, anxious to get to a safer place. Johanna, still watching those trees as they pass...
EXT. PLATEAU. HILL COUNTRY - AFTERNOON
Now they're out in the endless, windswept plateau again. A blasted earth as far as the eye can see. Kidd working hard to keep the horses moving. That damaged wheel groaning... Then a CLUNK.
(looking down at the wheel) Damn fixing's...
He stops the wagon. Gets out. Starts to work on the bolts. Johanna sits, wind in her face. Then gets out the wagon. Kidd watches as she walks ahead. A tiny figure under a big sky.
Not too far.
See Johanna, looking intently around. She begins singing quietly. Keeps walking. As though retracing old steps.
We have to go... (in Kiowa) Getting dark soon.
She ignores him. Eventually she reaches a track, leading away to the left. Kidd gets back in the wagon. Starts after her. Sees the track too. Johanna still singing.
We're just gonna stay on the main road now, you hear?
She looks at him. Stops singing. He makes the sign of the straight line.
(in Kiowa) Not safe. Here.
She shakes her head. And points down the track.
(in Kiowa) This way.
We haven't got time for this.
She looks at him. Points again.
(in English) We go.
Kidd knows what this is. He's been dreading it for days. She walks off down the track.
EXT. APPROACH TO SEARCHERS CABIN - AFTERNOON
Kidd out in the vast open space. Johanna walking ahead. Down a small incline, which reveals in the distance: An abandoned wooden shack.
EXT. SEARCHERS CABIN - AFTERNOON
Close on the shack as they approach. Everything about this - the wide open space, the sky reminds us of something. Something deeply rooted in our imagination. The opening of The Searchers.
EXT. SEARCHERS CABIN - CONTINUOUS
Kidd watches as ahead. Johanna stops. The wind buffeting her. The shack ahead. Ruined. To one side. The skeleton of a wagon. She looks at the door, flapping in the wind.
You don't have to go in there. We can still leave.
Beat on Johanna.
(in English)
She enters.
INT. SEARCHERS CABIN - CONTINUOUS

Johanna enters the shack. Kidd after her. A plain living area. Broken furniture. Broken windows. Blackened by fire. And everywhere the signs of savage violence. Bullet marks. Arrows. And dark blood stains. Johanna moves through it. Kidd behind her. She goes to the end of the room. A door. Through it a small room. Shutters long since fallen from their hinges. The sounds of wind outside. Johanna looks around. Three beds: A double. A child's bed. A cot. She goes to the child's bed. Reaches down. Finds under an upturned drawer, the remains of a corn doll.
EXT. SEARCHERS CABIN - CONTINUOUS

Johanna, holding the doll, walks outside again. Looks around. Her eyes tell the story. This is where they ran, where they hid, where she was taken. Kidd looking at Johanna. Watching her remember it all. She turns to Kidd. As if she has found everything she needed.
(bravely) Mama... Papa... tot? Yes. They're dead.
See Johanna. Taking it in. A small nod. She walks up to Kidd. Takes his arm. Holds it close. On Kidd. Doesn't know what to say.
EXT. WAGON. PLATEAU - DUSK
Back in the wagon. Light fading fast. They start to climb. Johanna quiet.
He makes the sign of the line.
(in Kiowa, subtitled) Forward. (in English) Don't look back. You hear me? Li-an.
Yeah. Gotta stick to that line. (beat and then) That's how we deal with it. She shakes her head.

(in Kiowa, subtitled) (in English) Johanna. No run. (in Kiowa, subtitled) I always remember. It is in front and behind. (in German, subtitled) I live. (in Kiowa, subtitled) Unbroken. (in Kiowa, subtitled) We are together. (in English) Always.
He looks at her certainty. Her bravery. As they reach the top of the slope and start to go down...
Well I only know the straight

line... That's the only way I know. Suddenly, a CRACK! The wagon lurches violently, as the wheel finally gives way. Kidd tries to control the horses. But they panic as the wagon tips... Kidd can't hold it.
Johanna jump... Jump! She does. And at the last minute so does he.
As the cart tumbles forty feet into a hidden canyon. Smashing into the rocks below.
EXT. CANYON FLOOR - NIGHT

Kidd and Johanna scramble down the rocks. They reach the wreckage of the wagon. ONE HORSE DEAD. THE OTHER INJURED and in distress. Kidd has no choice. He shoots it. The canyon falls quiet. Kidd goes to the wagon. Salvages what he can. Food. Water. And last his wife's photo. Damaged but still intact. He turns back. Sees Johanna crouching over the dead horses. Marking their passing with a Kiowa ritual.
EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - NIGHT
They walk on, wrapped up against the bitter cold of the desert night.
EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - DAY
Under a desert sun, still they walk. An endless hard road.
EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - NIGHT
They shelter under a rock. Johanna sleeping. Kidd wakes her.
He lifts her on to her exhausted feet. Gives her the last biscuit.
EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - NIGHT
That never ceasing wind. Kidd leading her. Johanna tucked behind his body for shelter. They stumble forward. Exhausted.
EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - MIDDAY SUN

They lie in a tiny pool of shade. Utterly spent. The last of the water gone. Kidd looks over at Johanna. Trying to find the words to prepare her. He stands. Lifts her into his arms. And keeps walking.
EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - LATE AFTERNOON

Still that wind. Kidd carrying Johanna now. Her eyes closed. Kidd reaching the edge of exhaustion. One small step after another... Suddenly, he sees something on the horizon. He tries to focus. Is that a horse? It looks like a horse. He takes a few steps towards it, but carrying Johanna is slowing him down. He gently lowers her to the ground.
He stumbles forwards. Towards the shape... Johanna opens her eyes.
(voice hoarse) Kep-tan?! Kidd is twenty yards ahead now.

The shape ahead of him dancing up a ridge... Kidd follows. Further and further from Johanna. Ahead: A LOW RUMBLING SOUND. Getting louder with every step. As it reaches the top of the ridge, the horse shape disperses. Just a DUST DEVIL. Kidd walks a few paces on. Still that building sound. He reaches the crest of the ridge. To reveal: A MASSIVE SANDSTORM. Roaring towards them through the gloom. Kidd turns back to Johanna. Sees her standing there alone.

He starts to run back to her... As the sandstorm engulfs them both. EXT. SOUTHERN PLAINS - CONTINUOUS A deafening tsunami of dust. Hammering into Kidd. He tries to shout but his voice is drowned out. He struggles back to where he left her... Near blind. Barely able to see two feet ahead. Throat filling with dust. Lungs suffocated. He searches. Desperate. Occasionally glimpsing the landscape ahead. Then he's in darkness. A phantom in the dust. He stumbles... Gets up, stumbles again... We watch him struggle to his feet. Desperate to find her. Then he starts to slow. Losing strength with every step. He falls to his knees. See despair in his eyes. Momentarily the dust clears a few yards ahead. He sees a rise in the ground. He crawls forward... Drives his hands into the dust. Heaving it away. Digging like an animal. With his last strength. And then he finds her. Curled up for protection. He drags her up. Frantically wipes her face. Pulls her into his chest.

And we see what this means to him. Then he looks up And sees to his horror, Through the gloom ahead... They're not alone. Ghostly shapes are moving towards them. Slowly they become clear. A BAND OF KIOWA INDIANS. Some on HORSEBACK and others on foot. Kidd pulls out his gun. But as they come closer he sees, They're not warriors. They're refugees. Looking for survival, just like them. Kidd lets the gun drop to the ground. Johanna walks forward towards them. Through the eddies of dust, Kidd watches as she talks with a GROUP OF KIOWA WOMEN. SEVERAL MEN join the group. Then an OLD MAN. THE CHIEF. Kidd sees Johanna climbs onto a horse. The Chief turns to look at him. Kidd watches as Johanna talks to the Chief. And then she turns, and rides back towards Kidd. She reaches him. Hands him water. As the tribe prepares to move on. As they leave, Kidd comes face-to-face with The Chief. A moment between them. A look of shared history. Of what might have been. And then the Kiowa disappear into the haze. Kidd climbs onto the horse. And they ride on.
EXT. PRAIRIE. NEAR CASTROVILLE - DAWN
Dawn breaks on a new day. Time has passed.
EXT. PRAIRIE. NEAR CASTROVILLE - DAY

The plateaus and cliffs of Hill Country have gone. They're in farm country now. DISTANT FIGURES toil in the fields. Backs bent to the ground. The people who built America. They approach a sign: Castroville. 5 miles. See Kidd. He knows their journey is almost done.
EXT. WAGON. CROSSROADS. APPROACH TO CASTROVILLE - DAY
Castroville comes into view.
See Johanna sensing a change.
(pointing ahead) We read story? Dime-ah? No. No more stories..
See Johanna, frowning.
No dime-ah? No dime-ah.
Johanna sees Castroville in the distance, slowly realising the truth...
No. Kep-tan. We go. Dime-ah! We go. We can't go dime-ah Johanna. This is your home. (in Kiowa) Your family. No. No!
It's where you belong.
She tries to get off the horse. Kidd holds her.
(beat and then) It's going to be alright.
They turn right, past a tumble-down shack. In the distance: a sparse farm house. "Leonberger", daubed onto a simple white sign. Johanna sees the farm. PEOPLE working the fields.
(as they near) Kep-tan, stay?
I can't. I can't stay with you.
(looking up at him) Kep-tan, go?
Where? (in Kiowa, subtitled) Where Kep-tan go? (in English) Tell me.
They stop outside the house. Knows he has to tell her.
This is what we're meant to do. You have to go home. (beat) And so do I.
Johanna's AUNT comes out of the door. Looks at them from afar.
(call out) Wilmhelm!
INT. LEONBERGER HOUSE - DAY
Kidd sat at a bare table. Johanna crouched in the corner of the room. Her UNCLE WILMHELM and AUNT AGNA sat across the table from Kidd.
She is like her mother. The hair. The same. Was?
(in German, subtitled)
(to her husband, in German, subtitled) Das Kind sieht wie Anna aus. (in English) My sister... she always went her own way. (MORE)
We said stay in Castroville. But she and her husband Wolf, they wanted to be out in the valley. Where the land is cheaper.
She looks across at her husband.
(in German, subtitled) Wolf. Er hort einfach nicht zu.
The Uncle nods.
So she thinks she is Indian now? Caught between perhaps. She needs time to adjust.
She must work.
(hating every moment) No doubt.
She must learn. (in German, subtitled) Wie wir zu leben.
(in English) The proper ways. Ja...
Kidd looks over at Johanna. Her back is turned.
You know my sister, when we found her in the bedroom, they cut the
